title_clean: The 2022 Gen Z Screen Time Report: Sneak Peek
blurb: A newsletter preview of dcdx's 2022 Gen Z Screen Time Report, teasing survey findings on how young people relate to their phones and social feeds.

blurb: A New York Times follow-up on the 'Luddite Club' teens years later, tracking how their phone-free ethos held up -- or didn't -- once they reached college.
A CounterPunch essay arguing that social media as a coherent cultural project is collapsing, undone by algorithmic decay, ad saturation, and users' own exhaustion with the medium.
ambiguous_note: Title signals a strong argument about social media's decline, but the specific evidence or thesis isn't confirmable without the full text.

blurb: A New York Times interview with psychiatrist Anna Lembke, author of Dopamine Nation, on how compulsive engagement with phones, screens, and instant gratification is reshaping mental health.

An Atlantic essay arguing that Americans' political perceptions are increasingly shaped -- and distorted -- by algorithmic feeds, using the fight over 2025 voter-perception narratives as a case in point.
ambiguous_note: Title suggests a broad argument about algorithmic influence on politics; the URL implies a specific tie to a Trump-administration voter-perception debate that isn't fully confirmable from the title alone.

blurb: A profile of a Harvard graduate student organizing part of Gen Z's pushback against social media, situated within a wider youth-led movement toward digital detox and reclaimed attention.
ambiguous_note: The specific individual and movement aren't named in the title, so details are inferred from context.

blurb: An interactive tracker from the Anxious Generation campaign mapping which U.S. states have passed policies restricting phones in schools or regulating youth social media use.
ambiguous_note: Inferred as a policy-tracking map tied to Jonathan Haidt's Anxious Generation campaign based on the URL and title.

blurb: A first-person op-ed from a young person who grew up on social media explaining why she's protesting Meta during Children's Mental Health Week, and what she wishes platforms had done differently.
A BBC report examining whether Gen Z has become dependent on short-form video and doomscrolling, and what that says about attention, mood, and digital habits.
An author archive collecting Behavioral Health News pieces by youth mental health advocate Sophie Szew, spanning her writing on adolescent wellbeing and the behavioral health system.
ambiguous_note: This is an author-archive listing rather than a single piece; the specific articles it links to aren't confirmable from the title alone.

blurb: A multi-country academic study examining the psychosocial factors -- peer pressure, risk perception, self-image -- that shape whether young people engage in sexting.

title_clean: The Common Sense Census: Media Use by Kids Zero to Eight
blurb: Common Sense Media's 2025 census of screen media use among children ages zero to eight, tracking how early exposure to devices and platforms has shifted over time.

title_clean: "Without It, I Wouldn't Be Here Today": LGBTQ+ Young People's Experiences in Online Spaces
blurb: A Hopelab story collection centering LGBTQ+ young people's own accounts of how online spaces and communities have been a lifeline for identity, connection, and survival.
Stanford's 2025 Youth Safety and Digital Wellbeing Report, assessing how well platforms, policies, and product design are protecting young people online.
A LinkedIn essay unpacking why Gen Z -- despite being digital natives -- remains especially susceptible to misinformation, and what platforms and educators could do about it.
ambiguous_note: A LinkedIn Pulse post with a generic framing; specific claims or data referenced in the piece aren't confirmable from the title alone.

title_clean: Fake News Is Fooling Young People -- and They Want It Stopped
blurb: A YPulse research brief finding that young people know fake news is a problem and are actively asking for stronger action against misinformation online.
ambiguous_note: YPulse is a youth-research firm, so this likely reflects survey data, but the specific study isn't named in the title.

title_clean: Health Promotion and the Digital Determinants of Health
blurb: An Oxford Academic paper arguing that digital platforms and algorithms function as social determinants of health in their own right, and proposing how health promotion should account for them.
Cory Doctorow's book making the case that surveillance capitalism can be dismantled through interoperability, antitrust action, and technical countermeasures -- not just regulation.
Yascha Mounk's Persuasion interview with Taiwan digital minister Audrey Tang on how democracies should handle misinformation without resorting to top-down censorship.
A newsletter update reflecting on nearly two years of data from a Social Media Index tracking project, summarizing how users' relationships with platforms have shifted since it launched.
ambiguous_note: The substack link gives no author or publication context, so the project behind the 'Social Media Index' and its specific findings are inferred from the title alone.

title_clean: Meta Tests New Restrictions for Instagram Teen Accounts
blurb: A Washington Post report on Meta's test of new Instagram teen-account restrictions, circulated here through youth advocacy pushback on the platform's safety claims.

blurb: An Accountable Tech report finding that Instagram's teen account safety features fall short in practice, documenting the harmful content and design patterns teens still encounter.
An NBC News report on Texas's advancing legislation to broadly restrict minors' access to social media, part of a growing wave of state-level platform regulation.

title_clean: Protecting the Developing Mind in a Digital Age: A Global Policy Imperative
blurb: An academic policy paper arguing that safeguarding children's cognitive and emotional development in the digital age requires coordinated global regulation, not just platform-level fixes.
A Black Girl Narrative piece exploring how love, kinship, and everyday resistance show up in Black girls' digital lives, reframing online space as a site of care rather than only risk.

title_clean: Rural Realities: Young People, Digital Technology, and Well-Being
blurb: A Hopelab research report showing that rural young people face distinct digital-wellbeing challenges -- from limited connectivity to social isolation -- that differ from their urban and suburban peers.
Snapchat's own 2025 year-in-review roundup, packaging user trends and platform highlights into a promotional recap for its audience.

blurb: Pew Research Center's 2025 survey of American teens on how social media and AI chatbots now factor into their daily routines, relationships, and sense of trust online.
